body.u: Im I'm now knocking 60, ImI'm quite wealthy I haven’t had too many ups and downs and IveI've got quite a healthy sum of money. I left school when I was 16 and went straight into a job quite a good job in fact it was an apprentice motor mechanic, a recognised trade in that day. My first wages was £25.00 in which I gave my mother £7.00 for board, with the £18 I had left I put a deposit down for an electric Bass guitar, the guitar cost £335.25 and it was an excellent bass for the money. My wages were coming in every week and I had to make sacrifices such as going out and such like just to buy this bass. After about a year I had paid up the money and had a 100 watt amplifier given to me for Christmas. Time was going fast and I practised every single hour of my spare time.

I was just coming up to 20 when I finished my apprenticeship as a motor mechanic. I became quite good on the bass and then was the first real breakthrough. I had decided to form a group calling it Wreackage. I went around Nitenight clubs and working men’s clubs looking for different talents on different instruments. I found a boy by the name of Kev Harrison and asked him if he would join my group and he said yes. Me on the bass and Kev on lead, a friend of Kevs tony played drums and had a good drum kit a Ludwig I think. We auditioned a boy called Roger who played Rhythm Guitar and Tony on drums. We instantly started to record songs and rehearse for rehearsals we used the old dockyard church where I was inspired for my first song I wrote called Broken down.

We also had learn’tlearnt some Queen, Quo, numbers and started off looking for booking. I got us two bookings over the Queen Phillipa and Kev got is one over the V.C. The very first nite over the Phillipa we had developed technical troubles with the P.A. system so we had to sing through the amps. The place was packed and everybody wanted more they cheered and cheered that night we got £50 to be shared out and with what we had left we spent on new gadgitsgadgets to make us more amasingamazing. We were popular all over Sheppey and word got around {illegible} Kent and we were playing to huge crowds. One day Wreakage had an argument and we just split just out of the blue split the end of Wreakage the end of a cult. After the groups had split I had just forgot about playing bass and just went on with my normal occupation as a motor mechanic quite a nice job really.
